<mxGeometry height="80" width="80" x="385" y="450" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="D_T3D19grgrDn62JJbPC-25" parent="1" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="Imbalance is in untested islander, K." vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="60" width="90" x="190" y="460" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="D_T3D19grgrDn62JJbPC-32" edge="1" parent="1" source="D_T3D19grgrDn62JJbPC-28" style="edgeStyle=orthogonalEdgeStyle;rounded=0;orthogonalLoop=1;jettySize=auto;html=1;exitX=1;exitY=0.5;exitDx=0;exitDy=0;entryX=1;entryY=0.5;entryDx=0;entryDy=0;" target="D_T3D19grgrDn62JJbPC-30"> <mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="D_T3D19grgrDn62JJbPC-28" parent="1" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;" value="Imbalance can be determined from comparing current outcome with previous tests where I and J were on seesaw. This is&nbsp;<b>idea</b>&nbsp;(2.)" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="130" width="135" x="540" y="460" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="D_T3D19grgrDn62JJbPC-30" parent="1" style="text;html=1;whiteSpace=wrap;strokeColor=none;fillColor=none;align=center;verticalAlign=middle;rounded=0;" value="<b>EXAMPLE</b>: In Test 2 (IJ vs KA) if the RIGHT half fell toward the ground, you know IJ is light or KA is heavy. In Test 3 (I vs J), if the RIGHT half falls, it is therefore because I is light. Likewise, if the LEFT half falls, it is because J is light. The same logic can be applied to if Test 2 has the LEFT half falling. In this case, instead of I or J being light, they must be heavy." vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="80" width="510" x="170" y="590" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> <mxCell id="D_T3D19grgrDn62JJbPC-58" parent="1" style="text;whiteSpace=wrap;html=1;align=left;" value="<p>Test 1 will always be a 4v4.</p> <p>This follows <strong>idea</strong> (1.). 4v4 is the right choice here rather than 5v5,<br> because the outcome of the 5v5 could leave you with 10 potential imbalances and only two tests remaining, which is not<br> solvable. Likewise, 6v6 is not advisable, as it will not reveal anything you do not already know. You already know the<br> imbalance is somewhere in the set of 12.</p>" vertex="1"> <mxGeometry height="190" width="225" x="475" y="10" as="geometry" /> </mxCell> </root> </mxGraphModel> </diagram> </mxfile>

div.div: Imbalance lies in set IJKL. Take 3 of the 4 potential, add a safe individual, and test. This decision pulls from ideas (1.) and (4.)

div.div: Imbalance can be determined from comparing current outcome with previous tests where I and J were on seesaw. This is idea (2.)

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 508px; height: 1px; padding-top: 620px; margin-left: 1px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: EXAMPLE: In Test 2 (IJ vs KA) if the RIGHT half fell toward the ground, you know IJ is light or KA is heavy. In Test 3 (I vs J), if the RIGHT half falls, it is therefore because I is light. Likewise, if the LEFT half falls, it is because J is light. The same logic can be applied to if Test 2 has the LEFT half falling. In this case, instead of I or J being light, they must be heavy.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 223px; height: 1px; padding-top: 7px; margin-left: 307px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.p:
This follows idea (1.). 4v4 is the right choice here rather than 5v5,
because the outcome of the 5v5 could leave you with 10 potential imbalances and only two tests remaining, which is not
solvable. Likewise, 6v6 is not advisable, as it will not reveal anything you do not already know. You already know the
imbalance is somewhere in the set of 12.
